coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from denatured sour milk and its multifunctional applications in the fluorometric determination of gold ions, in vitro bioimaging and fluorescent polymer film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Arumugam, Selva Sharma
Xuing, Jing
Viswadevarayalu, Annavaram
Rong, Yawen
Sabarinathan, Devaraj
Ali, Shujat
Agyekum, Akwasi Akomeah
Li, Huanhuan
Chen, Quansheng
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, the multifunctional applications of food waste derived carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been demonstrated. The results obtained from the absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis affirmed the superior photophysical property of the prepared CQD. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infra-red spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies further confirmed the crystallographic phase and surface functional groups present in the CQD. The ultrafine size of the CQD is established from the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrographs with the grain size of 2−4 nm. The excellent fluorescence property of the CQD is utilised to detect trace levels of Au3+ ions by simply mixing aqueous dispersion of CQD with ascorbic acid (AA). The sensing mechanism is based on the reduction of Au3+ to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by AA and the subsequent AuNPs induced fluorescence quenching of CQD by synergistic static quenching and inner filter effect. The CQD/AA probe could selectively detect Au3+ ion as low as 0.95 μM. The cytotoxicity of the CQD toward U-251 MG glioblastoma cell revealed that the CQD did not possess any significant cytotoxicity. The application of CQD in bioimaging of cells is ascertained by fluorescence microscopic analysis. Furthermore, fluorescent polymer films were prepared by incorporating CQD within the polymer matrix (poly-vinyl-alcohol).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-quantum dot
Food waste
Fluorescent sensing
Gold ions
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Practical application of the sensing probe
Evaluation of the photophysical properties of CQD
Crystal structure, morphology and composition of the CQD
CQD as fluorescent probe for Au3+ ions
Selectivity of the CQD fluorescent probe
Fluorescent ink and polymer thin film

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme depicting the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQD/AA probe in the presence of gold ions.
Absorption spectra of CQD (Inset: Aqueous dispersion of CQD under visible light and UV light illumination).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD at different excitation wavelengths and Excitation spectrum of CQD obtained at an λemi =430 nm and (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD at different pH conditions.
(A) XRD profile, (B) FT-IR spectrum obtained for the powdered CQD. (e) HR-TEM micrograph of the CQD/AA aqueous dispersion (Inset: Size distribution histogram).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CQD, High resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s peaks.

(A) Fluorescence profiles of the CQD/AA probe in the presence of different Au3+ ion concentrations: (1) 0, (2) 10, (3) 20, (4) 30, (5) 50, (6) 60, (7) 70, (8) 80, (9) 90, (10) 100, (11), 110, (12) 120, (13) 130, (14) 140 and (15) 150 μM; (B) The calibration curve for the CQD/AA probe in the presence of Au3+ ions.

Absorption spectra of (1) CQD alone and CQD/AA probe in the presence of different Au3+ ion concentrations: (2) 0, (3) 10, (4) 20, (5) 30, (6) 50, (7) 60, (8) 70, (9) 80, (10) 90, (11) 100 and (12) 110 μM (Inset: Shows the enlarged region from 300-800 nm).
Influence of inner-filter effect on the fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CQD/AA probe in the presence of increasing concentrations of Au3+ ions.
Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of CQD/AA probe under different concentrations of Au3+ ions.
Fluorescence decay curves of CQD/AA probe in the absence and presence of Au3+ ions.

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of CQD/AA probe-Au3+ system in the presence of various commonly interfering metal ions. (B) The fluorescence changes of CQD-coated filter paper upon the addition of Au3+ ions (0, 50 and 150 μM) under UV illumination (from left to right).

(A) Cell viability of U-251 MG glioblastoma cell upon treatment with CQD at varying concentrations. Confocal microscopic images of 251 MG glioblastoma cell treated with 50 μg mL−1 CQD for 72 h before (B) and after excitation (C). (Images taken under 405 nm excitation wavelength).
CQD embedded polymer PVA film under (A, C) daylight and (B, D) 365 nm UV light; Words written on butter paper using CQD fluorescent ink under (E) daylight and (F) 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from denatured sour milk and its multifunctional applications in the fluorometric determination of gold ions, in vitro bioimaging and fluorescent polymer film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, the multifunctional applications of food waste derived carbon quantum dots (CQD) have been demonstrated. The results obtained from the absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis affirmed the superior photophysical property of the prepared CQD. X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infra-red spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies further confirmed the crystallographic phase and surface functional groups present in the CQD. The ultrafine size of the CQD is established from the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrographs with the grain size of 2−4 nm. The excellent fluorescence property of the CQD is utilised to detect trace levels of Au3+ ions by simply mixing aqueous dispersion of CQD with ascorbic acid (AA). The sensing mechanism is based on the reduction of Au3+ to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by AA and the subsequent AuNPs induced fluorescence quenching of CQD by synergistic static quenching and inner filter effect. The CQD/AA probe could selectively detect Au3+ ion as low as 0.95 μM. The cytotoxicity of the CQD toward U-251 MG glioblastoma cell revealed that the CQD did not possess any significant cytotoxicity. The application of CQD in bioimaging of cells is ascertained by fluorescence microscopic analysis. Furthermore, fluorescent polymer films were prepared by incorporating CQD within the polymer matrix (poly-vinyl-alcohol).

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the advent of wide spread urbanization have led to a considerable demand for rapid, low-cost, highly selective and sensitive detection of various toxic chemicals and biologically important molecules in various environmental and food samples [1]. Previously, several methods including atomic adsorption spectrometry (AAS), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and synchrotron radiation X-ray spectrometry (SRXRS) have been developed for detection of several toxic chemicals [2–5]. However, most of these methods are relatively time-consuming, expensive and require complex sample treatment and sophisticated instruments.

In order to solve the above problems, spectrofluorometric based determination of toxic chemicals has been touted as a viable alternative. Among the various reported methods, fluorescence-based detection assay is by far the dominant detection method in the field of sensing technology due to several well-established advantages, such as rapid signal acquisition, sensitivity and localized luminescent signal [1,6]. So far, researchers have utilized various conventional fluorophores including organic dyes, fluorophore proteins (FPs) and semiconductor quantum dots for sensing and bio-imaging applications [7–9]. FPs are considered to be ideal tools for bio-imaging, especially for studying subjects like cellular organelles, cells, and tissues. The major advantages of FPs over other probes are that they can be genetically coded and labelled by one step. However, FPs have disadvantages as well namely the available number of FPs is still limited, the size of FPs is usually larger than that of organic dyes and their ability to undergo oligomerization may result in cytotoxicity [7]. In contrast, organic dyes are excellent marker tools for imaging and sensing applications as they can be easily conjugated to antibodies and various other biomolecules. Nevertheless, organic dyes are prone to undergo aggregation and possess low quantum yield [7,10,11]. In addition, factors such as limited extinction coefficients of traditional organic dyes and also low dye-to-reporter molecule labelling ratio resulted in a low detection limit in the fluorescence sensing assays [7]. Semiconducting quantum dots are superior in terms of their photo-physical properties and their unique core-shell structure makes them easy to be modified for optical sensing applications [7,12,13]. However, they are composed of heavy metal elements, which make them less compatible to the biosystems [7]. To address these concerns, enormous work has been done to improve the performance of the existing fluorescent probes.

The quest for new fluorescent probes that have multifunctional abilities and devoid of commonly observed paucity (such as low quantum yield, toxicity etc) have led to the discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQD) in the early 2006 [15]. Since then, CQD have attracted great deal of attention from researchers across different fields owing to their fascinating properties such as high-water solubility, excellent photostability, characteristic excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility [1,14]. The preparation of fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots requires expensive precursors, complex equipment or tedious treatment processes [1,14]. In contrast, high quantum yield CQD can be prepared from carbon-rich precursors. Recently, size-controllable and cost-effective methods have been developed for the generation of CQD with varying compositions and morphology [1,14].

The sensing of various toxic metal ions such as Fe3+, Hg2+, Cr6+, Ag+ and Cu2+ by using CQD as a fluorescence probe have been widely reported [16]. In comparison, only a handful of works have been reported on the fluorescence sensing of Au3+ [16]. Gold is a widely used noble metal in various fields including catalysis, biology and medicine. However, gold species are known to induce adverse impact on the biological system. For example, Au3+ ions exhibit strong affinity toward DNA and several enzymes which in turn could result in damage to peripheral nervous system, liver and kidney [17–21]. It has been reported that gold salts are capable of inducing blood dyscriasis, cutaneous reactions, nephrotoxicity, bone marrow depression, etc. In another work, researchers found out that HAuCl4 in the concentration of 10−15 μmol in 1 mL of saline caused agglutination and haemolysis of erythrocytes [22]. As a result, accurate detection of Au3+ in biological and environmental systems assumes greater significance. Although some researchers have used CQD to detect Au3+ in biological and aqueous medium, the scope for further improvement still persist [21–24]. So far, most of the reported sensing assays are done by exploiting the non-specific interaction of Au3+ ions with the surface functional groups of the CQD or by utilising the inherent reducing ability of CQD to convert Au3+ ions to Au0. In general, such sensing assays suffer from lower selectivity, as these detection strategies are prone to get affected by other external factors such as pH, ionic strength and temperature etc. On the contrary, the use of sensing platform that relies on the reaction specific quenching can potentially improve the selectivity due to lower possibility of false positive signals.

The large-scale use of CQD in real life applications may lead to environmental and human exposure [1,14]. Owing to its high solubility in water, the CQD could easily accumulate in aquatic ecosystems and the human body (organs and tissues) [1,14]. Therefore, assessment of cytotoxicity of the CQD both in vivo and in vitro conditions are very important. The use of environmentally benign reaction conditions for CQD synthesis by utilising the naturally available carbon rich resources such as orange juice, lemon juice, banana juice, apple, gooseberry, Phyllanthus acidus fruit, Nescafe instant coffee, and coriander leaves have been widely reported [24–28]. In a recent report Raji et. al., expressed their qualms for choosing food materials to derive CQD amidst large food storage and starvation in different parts of the world [25]. Thus, the use of food wastes such as orange-peel, onion waste, vegetable and food wastes for the CQD preparation could be regarded as truly greener approach [25–30]. The advent of urbanisation has resulted in dumping of largescale municipal waste including food left-over within the urban areas. With the global amount of food waste already reached 1.3 billion tons and keep increasing dramatically, it has become a critical concern in many countries [32]. Consequently, many researchers have envisioned the use of food wastes such as beverage, bread pieces, cat feed stocks to prepare highly fluorescent CQD. For instance, Ahn et al., prepared CQD by one step hydrothermal carbonisation of hamburger sandwich leftover without any pre- or post-treatment process. In a similar way, researchers have prepared CQD by direct heating of 20 mL of carbonated soft drink at 170 °C for 30 min. However, the as-prepared CQD exhibited relatively lower emission performance. Subsequently, organic solvents such as ethanol and oleic acid were added to the precursor solution to improve the fluorescence property of the soft drink derived CQD. Of late, Park et.al., reported large-scale synthesis of CQD from bulk waste food materials in the presence of ethanol solvent (up to 100 Kg) using ultrasound irradiation at room-temperature [33]. Unlike the solid food wastes or soft drinks, denatured milk consists of nitrogen rich proteins. As a result, denatured milk offers a straight forward synthesis of CQD by one step hydrothermal method without the need for any organic solvents and complex pre- or post-treatment process. In the present investigation, denatured sour milk was utilized as a precursor to generate highly fluorescent blue emitting CQD by simple one-pot hydrothermal method and the resultant material is characterized by several analytical techniques. The prepared CQD were explored for multifunctional applications such as fluorescent probe for selective determination of Au3+, bio imaging, fluorescent ink and fluorescent polymer thin film. In addition, the biocompatibility of the CQD toward the U-251 MG glioblastoma cell line was investigated and reported here.

The preparation of CQD from the denatured milk was achieved by following the previously reported work with slight modifications [30,34]. Briefly, freshly pasteurized milk obtained from the Auchan supermarket, Zhenjiang, PR China was transferred into a sealed beaker and kept at room temperature for four days to undergo denaturation. The milk spoilage is further ascertained by the obvious decrease in pH value from 6.5 to 4.5 along with the visible curdle formation. The CQD were then generated by transferring 15 mL denatured milk into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then subjecting it to hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 3 h. At the end of the reaction, the autoclave is allowed to cool down naturally and the resultant dark yellow solution is subjected to centrifugation (10,000 rpm) to remove larger residues. The centrifuged solution is then filtered using a Millipore membrane (0.45 μm) and the filtrate was freeze dried to obtain solid CQD residues (∼140 mg). The obtained CQD powders was utilized to prepare the stock solution (1 mg mL−1).

The absorption and fluorescence spectral profiles of the aqueous dispersion of CQD were acquired using the Rayleigh UV–vis spectrophotometer and Cary-V-1000 spectrofluorometer, respectively. The fluorescence spectroscopic analysis was carried out at by exciting the samples at 350 nm with a scan speed of 50 nm min−1 within the wavelength range of 400−800 nm. The emission and excitation slit widths were maintained at 5 and 2 nm, respectively. Time resolved fluorescence spectroscopic analysis were carried out using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique using a Horiba Jobin Yvon equipped with pulsed-diode excitation source of 330 nm. The fluorescence decay data were analysed using IBH DAS6 software provided with the instrument. The quality of the fits was judged by analysing the chi-square values and the distribution of the residues. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the freeze dried CQD was acquired in the 2θ range of 10−80° at a scan rate of 2° min−1 using Bruker D8 PXRD diffractometer equipped with CuKα (λ = 1.5184 Å) radiation. Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Rayleigh) of the CQD was performed by means of KBr pellet technique. The chemical structure of the prepared CQD was analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB). The morphology of the CQD were analysed using HR-TEM (JEOL JEM 2100) at an operating voltage of 200 kV and the particle size was calculated using ImageJ software.

The fluorescence sensing experiments were performed by the standard addition method. Briefly, various concentration of the Au3+ ions was added to 3 mL aqueous solution containing 50 μL (16 μg/mL) of the CQD and 100 μL of 1 M ascorbic acid (0.03 M) (pH = 7). The solution was mixed thoroughly and kept at room temperature for 5 min and then transferred to a quartz cuvette for spectral analysis. The selectivity of the CQD probe toward the Au3+ ions, was evaluated by carrying out fluorescence titration experiments in the presence of common interfering metal ions (Cr6+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+ and K+). The sensing measurements were performed in triplicate at room temperature. Limit of detection (LOD) value was computed using the relation LOD = 3S/m, where S is the standard deviation of blank measurement (n = 10) and m is the slope of the calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The practical applicability of the developed CQD/AA probe toward Au3+ ions was analysed in drinking water sample collected from the Jiangsu University campus. The collected water samples were filtered three times through Whatman filter paper and spiked with known concentration of the Au3+ ions. The quantification assay was performed by the aforementioned procedure by recording the fluorescence profiles and subsequently analysing the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0). The following equation was used to calculate the rate of recovery Eq. (1),
(1)
R e c o v e r y
% =
C o n c .
o f
A u
3 +
i o n s
f o u n d
C o n c .
o f
A u
3 +
i o n s
a d d e d
× 100

The U-251 MG glioblastoma cell line was grown in culture flasks containing DMEM/F12 + 10 % FBS without antibiotics. Cells were maintained in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C in an incubator. In vitro cytotoxicity assessment of CQD against U-251 MG glioblastoma cell line was performed using CCK8 method. In brief, U-251 MG glioblastoma cells were seeded at 200 cells/well into 96-well culture plates at a total volume of 100 μL per well. A blank control group were placed with the same volume. After 24 h, requisite concentrations of CQD were added in 3 wells per concentration, following which the nanomaterial was incubated with the cells for 72 h. For CCK8 measurement, 10 μL CCK8 solution was added to the corresponding wells, two hours later, the optical density (OD) of the well containing the CCK8 solution was measured on a microplate reader at 450 nm.

Following the incubation, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in situ for 5 min at room temperature. The cells were then washed thrice with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and were imaged using a fluorescent microscope (Nikon A1R MP + confocal microscopy (Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) at 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the photophysical properties of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of the waste milk derived CQD was examined by absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , the absorption spectra of the CQD exhibits a sharp band at 290 nm, which is ascribed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring structure [1,14,25,30]. The shoulder absorption band located within the wavelength region of 300∼400 nm is indicative of the n → π* transition. It has been reported that n → π* transition results from the edge states located on the boundary of the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and the electron transition of the surface functional groups [31]. The inset of Fig. 1 shows that under visible light the diluted CQD dispersed solution appeared pale yellow in colour, while under UV light illumination (365 nm) the solution generated a bright fluorescence, which implies the formation of highly fluorescent CQD.

The wavelength dependent fluorescence properties of the CQD are analysed under the different excitation wavelength and the resulting spectral profiles are displayed in Fig. 2 A. It can be seen that the with the change in the excitation wavelength (310−410 nm) the emission maxima and the fluorescence intensity of the CQD showed a continuous red shift. Interestingly, at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the CQD exhibited the maximum fluorescence intensity at 430 nm. To rule out the possibility of fluorescence contribution from the precursor solution, fluorescence analysis was done for the precursor solution (Fig. 2). As depicted in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence spectrum of the precursor shows no obvious peak, indicating that the blue fluorescence actually originates from the CQD. Moreover, the denatured milk solution was subjected to visible and UV light illumination (365 nm) (Fig. S1a). It is obvious that the colour of the spoiled milk appeared pale white under both conditions. The absence of characteristic fluorescence suggests that the observed optical property originates from the CQD and not from the precursor solution. As shown in Fig. 2A, the excitation spectra of CQD exhibit two excitation peaks at 244 nm and 340 nm, which could be attributed to π→π* of aromatic CC bonds and n→π* transition of various heteroatomic functional groups covered on the surface of CQD, respectively. In order to unveil the excitation/relaxation pathway in the CQD, excitation-emission matrix spectroscopic analysis was carried out (Fig. S1b). As depicted in Fig. S1b, the 2D topographical contour map of the CQD exhibits two emission centres located at 420 (λex =240 nm) and 425 nm (λex =340 nm). It was pertinent to note that the two fluorescence maximum centres are located almost in the same wavelength region irrespective of the excitation centres i.e. from 240 nm (corresponding to the CQD core) or the 340 nm (corresponding to the surface defects of CQD). It is obvious that when photons illuminate the CQD, the excitons that are generated from the carbogenic core are transitioned and accumulated in the adjacent surface defect traps and return to the ground state to generate blue emission. Further, at the longer wavelength region of the 2D contour map a slight elongation was observed which could be ascribed to the tail-like structure observed at the red edge of the excitation dependent emission spectra of the CQD. It has been well established that owing to the surface defects and quantum confinement effect, CQD in general are known to exhibit excitation dependent properties [1,14,25,30].

The quantum yield (QY) of CQD is evaluated relative to the standard quinine sulphate which is having a QY of ∼54 % in 0.1 N sulfuric acid. The computed quantum yield value of CQD was found to be 13 %, which is in good agreement with the previously reported carbon-based fluorescence materials [30,35]. The stability of the materials is one of the key parameters to realise practical applications. The fluorescence stability of the CQD were analysed under different ionic strength, photoirradiation time, pH and long-term storage conditions. It can be seen that at varying ionic strengths, the fluorescence intensity of CQD remained same with no significant alterations (Fig. S2A). Moreover, under continuous photoirradiation at 365 nm for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity of CQD showed significant stability (Fig. S2B). As shown in Fig. 2B, the change in pH has a marked influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CQD. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of CQD was perturbed under higher acidic and basic pH conditions. The fluorescence intensity of CQD showed a very similar behaviour within the pH 4−8. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of CQD remain almost same even after 60 days of storage. All the above-mentioned results indicate that CQD are highly stable and it can retain the fluorescence properties for longer period of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure, morphology and composition of the CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallographic pattern, surface functional groups and morphology of the CQD were evaluated using XRD, FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis. Fig. 3 A depicts the XRD peaks obtained for the lyophilized CQD powder. It is obvious that the Bragg peaks located at the diffraction angle (2θ) namely 17.85, 21.65 and 26.33° could be indexed to the (004), (101), and (002) crystal planes of the graphitic carbon and intercalated graphite as per the JCPDS file no 46-0870 and 04- 0221, respectively [36,37]. Similar kind of XRD patterns have been reported for the carbon onions and food derived carbon nanoparticles [36,37]. It is pertinent to note that the XRD pattern is characterized by well-defined peaks corresponding to the graphitic phase and the absence of other noticeable peaks suggest that the CQD is devoid of any other phase impurities. Moreover, the obtained XRD pattern is in good agreement with the previously reported work [30,34,36,37].

FTIR analysis was performed to gain insights into the functional groups present in the CQD. The FTIR profile of the CQD displayed in Fig. 3B exhibits a prominent peak around 3225 cm−1 corresponding to the NH and OH stretching vibrations. The appearance of weak bands around 2961 and 2869 cm−1 could be attributed to the CH3 and CH2 stretching vibrations, while the presence of peaks at 2360, 1670, 1445 and 1110 cm−1 corresponds to the atmospheric CO2, CO, NH and CO functional groups, respectively. It has been well documented that the milk is a rich source of carbon, in the forms of carbohydrates and proteins. When denatured sour milk is subjected to hydrothermal treatment, the protein and carbohydrates gets fragmented into smaller amino acid residues resulting in protein denaturation. Finally, the fragmented amino acid residues, carbohydrates and other carbonaceous materials undergo a series of reactions such as ring opening, polymerization and carbonization to yield ultrafine CQDs. Since protein and amino acids are rich source of nitrogen and sulphur moieties, the obtained CQDs are expected to contain nitrogen, sulphur and other carboxylic functional groups [34]. The morphology and the particle size distribution analysis of the CQD were done by HR-TEM measurements. Fig. 3C shows the HR-TEM micrograph of the CQD. It is apparent that the CQD are well-dispersed with a uniform size distribution of about 2−4 nm. The presence of quasi spherical isolated particles clearly indicates the existence of stable surface stabilisation which is afforded by negatively charged functional groups.

XPS analysis was carried out to delineate the chemical composition and nature of bonding in CQD. It can be seen that the XPS survey spectrum of CQD presented in Fig. 4 a exhibits three peaks corresponding to C1s (76.16 %), N1s (6.32 %), and O1s (17.52 %) at 284, 400 and 530 eV, respectively which implies that CQD defects mainly contain N and O. Fig. 4(b–d) shows the detailed peak fitting analysis of the high resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4b, the C1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to sp3 carbons (CC/CH) at 284.30 eV, sp2 carbons (CC) at 285.31 eV, and carbonyl carbons (CO) and carboxyl carbons (COOH) at 287.50 eV. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum displayed in Fig. 4c establishes the presence of two bonding states namely, C-NH2 (397.45 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (398.03 eV). As shown in Fig. 4d, the O1s peak can be resolved into two components located at 529.29 and 530.63 eV, representing the existence of CO and COH groups, respectively [38–40]. The XPS results indicate that the surface of the as-synthesized CQD is functionalized by multiple oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQD as fluorescent probe for Au3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of CQD toward metal ions were analysed in the presence of AA and the obtained results were displayed in Fig. S3A. It is evident that the addition of Au3+ ion evoked a significant fluorescence quenching, as compared to the other metal ions. This particular fluorescence signal response of CQD/AA to Au3+ ion could be ascribed to the reduction of Au3+ to Au° by AA. It is believed that the formation of AuNPs would initiate a quenching effect due to the significant spectral overlap with the fluorescence spectral profile of CQD/AA probe. In the designing of an efficient fluorescent probe several key parameters such as the working concentration of CQD, AA, incubation time and pH conditions have to be optimised. In the present study we have used a lower concentration of CQD (16 μg/mL) as the optimum amount to perform the sensing assay. It has been well documented that the use of lower concentration of fluorophore will afford greater sensitivity while the use of higher concentration will result in broader detection range [41]. Thus, considering the two main factors namely sensitivity (limit of detection (LOD)) and linear detection range, we opted for a relatively lower concentration of CQD. Further, the ideal conditions for the sensing of Au3+ ions were determined by studying the effect of AA concentration. It was found out that the addition of lower concentration of AA had a negligible influence on the fluorescence property of the CQDs. However, upon the addition of much higher concentration of AA (30 mM) the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs decreased substantially (Fig. S3B). The observed decrease in the fluorescence intensity could be ascribed to the combined photo induced electron transfer (PET) and the hydrogen-bonding interactions that exist between the CQDs and AA. Zhou et al., reported a similar behaviour in the case of amine-modified carbon quantum dots. They proposed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of amine-modified carbon quantum dots in the presence of AA is initiated by both PET and the hydrogen-bond interactions [42]. The concentration of AA had a pronounced effect on the reduction of Au3+ ion to Au0. It is observed that when the concentration of AA is fixed at 0.03 M, the reduction of Au3+ ion is complete. The sensing performance of CQD/AA probe toward Au3+ ion under different pH conditions ranging from 2 to 10 are investigated and the results are displayed in Fig. S4A. It is seen that the relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of CQD/AA probe in the presence of Au3+ ion was found to be minimum in the pH range of 6.0–7.0 (The term F0 and F corresponds to the relative corrected fluorescence intensities of CQD/AA probe in the absence and presence of the Au3+). This could be attributed to the fact that at neutral pH the AA mediated reduction of Au3+ ion to Au° is complete. This in turn could evoke maximum quenching in CQD/AA probe. The kinetics of quenching process over a specified period of time was analysed and it turned out that 10 min incubation time is sufficient to observe complete perturbation in the fluorescence signal of CQD/AA probe, which clearly indicates the ability of the probe to detect Au3+ rapidly without any strict time control (Fig. S4B). Thus, to achieve higher sensitivity we opted for the aforementioned conditions to perform the sensing assay.

To elucidate the fluorescence sensing application of CQD/AA probe, steady state fluorescence titration experiments were carried out and the resultant data is displayed in Fig. 5A. As depicted in Fig. 5A, in the presence of AA, aqueous dispersion of CQD exhibits a fluorescence maximum at 430 nm, upon excitation at 350 nm. The Au3+ sensing assay was performed by carrying out fluorescence titration experiments of CQD/AA probe in the presence of varying concentrations of Au3+ ions. It can be seen that upon the addition of incremental addition of Au3+ ions the fluorescence intensity of CQD/AA probe showed a marked decrement without any significant shift in the emission maxima. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity correlates well with the concentration of the Au3+ ions. Subsequently, the data derived from the emission titration experiments were utilised to obtain a calibration plot between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration of the Au3+ ions. It is evident from Fig. 5B that the calibration plot showed a good linearity within the investigated Au3+ ions concentration. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.95 μM, which is comparable to the previously reported values for the fluorometric determination of Au3+ ions (Table S1).

It has been previously reported that the Au3+ induced fluorescence quenching of CQD are known to take place by several mechanisms, namely by reductive electron transfer from CQD to Au3+, coordination-induced aggregation and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Au3+ [21]. The absence of notable perturbations to the fluorescence intensity of CQD upon the addition of Au3+ clearly rules out the possibility of reductive electron transfer or metal ion induced aggregation. Interestingly, in the present case the fluorescence quenching of CQD/AA probe could be ascribed to the AA mediated reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs and the subsequent occurrence of IFE or FRET. To ascertain the formation of AuNPs, the absorption spectral analysis of CQD/AA probe in the presence of varying concentration of Au3+ ion was carried out and the resulting spectral data is displayed in Fig. 6. It is apparent that in the absence of AA, the absorption spectral profile of CQD exhibits a sharp band at 290 nm. Upon the addition of AA, the absorption intensity of CQD showed an enhancement with the appearance of two strong peaks. The weak band centered at 250 nm is ascribed to the AA, whereas the peak at 280 nm corresponds to the CQD. It has been previously reported that for the mixture of AA and amine-CQDs, the characteristic absorption peak of AA at 259 nm is red shifted to 266 nm. Subsequently, the absorption intensity of amine-CQDs at 286 nm showed an increase with the maximum peak got split into two weak peaks [42]. Thus, the observed results are in good agreement with the previously reported work and it clearly indicates the existence of weak interactions between AA and CQD. It is well known that AA greatly absorbs ultraviolet-light and it is evident from Fig. 6 . Upon the addition of increasing concentration of Au3+, the absorption peak around 230 nm showed an increase. This increase is more pronounced at higher concentration of Au3+, this could be attributed to the increased molar absorptivity of AA at 230 nm. It has been reported that with the decrease in pH value (1.50–4.75) the molar absorptivity of AA at 223 nm increases and varies between 4200 and 1700 dm3 mol−1 cm−1. Interestingly, at this pH condition the molar absorptivity of AA at 265 nm decrease considerably which varies from nil to 2600 dm3 mol−1 cm−1 [43]. Thus, the noticeable changes at 230 nm could be ascribed to the decrease in pH. Moreover, the dissociation of Au3+ (tetrachloroauric acid) and the subsequent reduction of Au3+ to Au° could have further contributed to the lowering of pH, thereby resulting in the enhancement in the molar absorptivity of the AA present in the solution.It is obvious from Fig. 6 that upon the addition of increasing concentration of Au3+, a broad band started to appear around 400−800 nm with the absorption maximum centered around 580 nm. Moreover; the initial pale-yellow colour of the CQD/AA probe resulted in visible colour change and the absorption spectral evolution was found to be strongly dependent on the concentration of the Au3+ ion. Interestingly, the observed colour change is dependent on the concentration of the added Au3+, suggesting that in addition to CQD/AA, the concentration of Au3+ also play a significant role in the visible colour change. It is pertinent to note that colour change is associated with the subsequent precipitation of large agglomerated Au clusters. Additionally, the results obtained from the absorption spectral studies further revealed the formation of broad band in the higher wavelength region between 500−800 nm. It has been previously reported that the formation of broad band at higher wavelength region could be ascribed to the coupling of localized surface plasmon bands of AuNPs [44]. Thus, the formation of broad band implies the formation of larger sized AuNPs. To further ascertain the formation of aggregated AuNPs clusters, HR-TEM analysis was carried out for the mixture comprising of CQD/AA and Au3+ ions. It is obvious that the micrograph displayed in Fig. S5 clearly shows the presence of aggregated AuNPs. Further, it can be seen that the CQD particle coalesced together to form a carbon matrix around the aggregated AuNPs. This observation further complements the findings derived from the absorption spectral analysis.

It is known that AA at an optimum concentration could readily reduce Au3+ halides to metals and in addition to its reducing ability AA can form a relatively stable complex at the surface of the metal particles. In fact, AA has the ability to reduce and form bidentate complexes with other metals such as Cu(II) and Ag(I). However, in the presence of Au cations the reduction of other metal ions Cu(II) and Ag(I) by AA is not favoured because the reduction potential of Cu2+ and Ag+ is much lower than the AuBr4−, AuCl4−, and Au3+ ions [45]. Thus, the present strategy of using CQD/AA to detect Au3+ could be highly selective. It is well known that AA is a weak dibasic acid and depending on the pH conditions, AA can exist in its monoanion forms at pH 4–6 and dianion at pH 11−12. It must be recalled that the present sensing assay was performed in neutral aqueous conditions. It is believed that post addition of the Au3+ into the CQD/AA probe, the dissociation of tetrachloroauric acid could have lowered the pH to certain magnitude and this in turn could have contributed to the increased presence of AA monoanion species. It has been previously reported that the resonance arising out of the shared negative charge between the CO and O at positions 1 and 3, respectively makes AA monoanion as strong as citric acid [45]. Further, AA monoanion was reported to be more effective in replacing the Cl- ions in the gold halide complexes to form Au(III) complexes and its subsequent reduction to metallic gold. Thus, upon its addition to the aqueous dispersion of CQD/AA probe, Au3+ ion rapidly gets reduced to Au0 and the overall reduction reaction involved in the reaction is given below Eq. (2) [46] Scheme 1 ;
(2) 2HAuCl4 + 3C6H8O6 → 2Au0 + 3C6H6O6 + 8HCL

It should be noted that owing to the formation of Au NPs, the newly formed broad band around 400−800 nm resulted in a significant spectral overlap with the emission spectra of CQD. Such spectral overlap between the fluorophore and the quencher could lead to FRET or IFE based quenching mechanism. Therefore, to delineate the quenching process, the observed fluorescence intensity is first corrected for the IFE by employing the following relationship Eq. (3) [47]:
(3)
F
C o r r
=
F
O b s
×
e
(
A
e x i
+
A
e m i
)
2
where FObs correspond to the observed fluorescence intensity without any correction for IFE. FCorr is the corrected fluorescence intensities. Aexi and Aemi are the solution absorbance at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively.

Fig. 7 , shows the plot of observed and corrected fluorescence quenching of CQD/AA probe in the presence of Au3+ ions. It is apparent that the observed and corrected fluorescence intensities showed a progressive decrease in the presence of Au3+ albeit in the former case the quenching occurred faster than the latter, indicating that the Au NPs driven IFE has induced partial quenching of the CQD/AA. Moreover, the observed quenching in the IFE corrected fluorescence intensity ratio indicates the possibility of FRET, collisional or binding related quenching of CQD/AA probe.

To get further insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQD/AA probe, the quenching data were analysed using the Stern-Volmer equation Eq. (4) [47]:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
k
s v
where, F0 and F are the relative fluorescence intensities of CQD/AA probe in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. K
q is the bimolecular quenching rate constant; τ
0 is the average lifetime of the pristine CQD/AA probe in the absence of the quencher and [Q] gives the concentration of the quencher. K
sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant corresponds to the extent of quenching.

As shown in Fig. 8 , the Stern-Volmer plot obtained using the fluorescence intensity ratio of CQD/AA probe and the concentration of Au3+ clearly shows a linear plot with good correlation coefficient value of 0.99. The observation of a linear Stern-Volmer plot indicates the existence of either static or collisional quenching and this effectively ruled out the possibility of combined static-dynamic mechanism in which the Stern-Volmer plot would have produced a nonlinear upward curve. The slope value derived from the linear plot yielded the quenching constant value of 4428 mol−1 dm3. By utilising the average fluorescence lifetime (τ0) of pristine CQD/AA probe (6.9 ns) and the quenching constant value (4428 mol−1 dm3) the bimolecular quenching constant (kq) value is found to be 6.41 × 1011 mol−1 dm3 s−1 which is higher than the diffusion-controlled limit of 1010 mol−1 dm3 s−1 [47]. This observation clearly indicates that apart from the IFE, the ground state complex formation between the Au NPs and CQD/AA have significantly contributed to the quenching.

The existence of FRET and static quenching mechanism can be easily distinguished using lifetime measurements. The time resolved fluorescence analysis of CQD/AA probe before and after the addition of Au3+ were carried out (Fig. 9 ). It turned out that in the absence of Au3+ the life time data of CQD/AA probe is best fitted to a mono-exponential decay curve with an average life time of about 6.9 ns. It is to be noted that the addition of Au3+ to CQD/AA probe did not induce any significant perturbations in the average life time value and the obtained life time value 6.7 ns remained almost similar, indicating the absence of FRET mechanism in the quenching process. It has been well established that the existence of static ground-state complexes does not alter the decay time of the un-complexed fluorophores. Thus, in the present case the co-existence of both IFE and static mechanisms contributed to the quenching of CQD/AA probe in the presence of Au3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CQD fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the proposed sensing probe was evaluated by performing competitive assay experiments of Au3+ in the presence of other potential interfering metal ions. Briefly, for the blank readings a known quantity of Au3+ was added to the sensing probe (150 μM) and in the competitive assay experiment requisite quantity of Au3+ and another metal ion (30 μM) were mixed together with the CQD/AA probe individually to perform the fluorescence quenching measurements. As evidenced from Fig. 10 A, the presence of other interfering metal ions showed negligible influence on the fluorescence signal of the CQD/AA probe. The obtained results clearly indicate that the efficient fluorescence quenching effect observed upon the addition of Au3+ was not influenced in the presence of other metal ions and that the CQD/AA probe is selective toward Au3+ in the aqueous solution.

The excellent sensitivity and selectivity of CQD/AA probe toward Au3+ suggests that the proposed sensing assay could be applied to sense Au3+ in real field water samples. The collected samples were first centrifuged and filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove particulate matters. Then the water samples were spiked with known concentrations of Au3+ and analysed by the proposed sensing assay. The effect of Au3+ ions spiked water samples on the fluorescence response of the CQD/AA probe resembled that of the control experiments with significant recoveries and error limits (Table S2). It is apparent that the CQD/AA probe is able to detect Au3+ ions even in the presence of various minerals and organics present within the water samples. Further, to ascertain the practical applicability, we have used CQD to fabricate a paper-based sensing platform to detect Au3+ ions. The paper-based sensing platform was developed by soaking a commercially obtained Whatman paper in aqueous dispersion of CQD. The sensing assay was performed by adding an aliquot of Au3+ containing AA solution to the CQD-coated filter paper. As shown in Fig. 10B, under UV illumination the CQD-coated filter paper showed a bright blue fluorescence. Remarkably, upon the addition of two different concentrations of Au3+ containing AA solution, the blue fluorescence is quenched with varying degree of colour change. It is to be noted that the fabricated paper-based sensing platform makes use of untreated commercially obtained filter paper and showed remarkable response to the Au3+ ions.

It is obvious that the prepared CQD with its excellent fluorescence properties, greater stability in higher ionic strength and photostability at prolonged photoirradiation conditions, stands out as an ideal candidate for bioimaging applications. However, for biological applications the material should possess low cytotoxicity to cell lines. Therefore, the biocompatibility of the CQD toward the U-251 MG glioblastoma cell line was investigated under varying concentration of the CQD by CCK8 assay. Fig. 11 A shows the data derived from the CCK8 assay with increasing doses of the CQD (10−1000 μg/mL), it is observed that the CQD did not exhibit apparent toxicity toward U-251 MG glioblastoma cell. Interestingly, the cell viabilities were retained at 85 % even at a higher concentration of 1000 μg mL−1, denoting the exceptional biocompatibility. Further, the obtained results are in good agreement with the previously reported works on CQD [35,41,48]. Moreover, it is pertinent to note that at the CQD concentration of 50−200 μg mL−1, the cell viability analysis reveals almost a nil toxicity. This observation makes it obvious that the use of CQD within this concentration is highly conducive for bioimaging applications. The in vitro cellular uptake experiments were performed by incubating the 251 MG glioblastoma cell with 50 μg mL−1 CQD for 72 h. The cells were then imaged under a confocal laser scanning microscope and the resultant microscopic images are displayed in Fig. 11(B & C). It is obvious that in the absence of laser excitation no obvious emission was observed from the CQD treated cells, upon excitation at 405 nm the CQD treated cells exhibited blue emission. It is seen that the CQD glows are mostly confined to the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area whereas deep inside the nucleus only a marginal glow is observed. Similar kind of results have been reported for other blue emitting CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent ink and polymer thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent stable fluorescence properties of CQD was explored to other possible applications such as fluorescent polymer thin films and invisible fluorescent ink. To achieve this, 1 mL of CQD are added to preheated 50 mL aqueous solution containing 1 g of poly(vinyl alcohol), then the mixed solution was coated on to a thin glass plate and allowed to cool at room temperature to obtain fluorescent polymer thin film. Fig. 12 A–D shows the CQD embedded polymer film under visible and UV light. It is apparent that the obtained films are highly flexible and transparent under visible light. However, when subjected to UV light illumination the CQD polymer film showed bright fluorescence. It is to be noted that the blue fluorescence is uniformly distributed throughout the polymer film suggesting that the poly(vinyl alcohol) acted as a perfect host material by accommodating the CQD within its matrix [41]. The obtained solid-state fluorescence thin film could be utilised in various other applications such as solid-state lighting systems and smart packaging materials. Further, we extended the application of CQD in fluorescent ink. Briefly, a sketch pen is filled with aqueous dispersion of CQD. Fig. 12E & F depicts the words Jiangsu University written using the CQD ink filled sketch pen on to a commercial butter sheet. It is seen that under the visible light the written text appeared blank, while under the UV light illumination the text generated bright blue fluorescence. Remarkably, under ambient conditions the written text exhibited blue fluorescence even after 30 days of storage suggesting its potential use in security related communications and anticounterfeiting applications.

Herein, CQD were prepared from sustainable food waste by a simple one step method. The obtained CQD exhibits an ultrafine quasi spherical morphology with an average grain size of 2 nm. The photophysical studies revealed that the CQD showed excellent stability in varying pH conditions, ionic strength, possess good quantum yield and exhibited significant tolerance against photobleaching under continuous photo irradiation. The prepared CQD in combination with AA is then applied toward fluorescence sensing of Au3+. It was confirmed that the addition of Au3+ ions initiated the fluorescence quenching. of CQD/AA probe by means of synergistic static quenching process and IFE effect. The CQD/AA probe exhibited good selectivity toward Au3+ ions over other common interfering metal ions with a wide linear concentration range of 10−150 μM. The practical applicability of the proposed assay was demonstrated by fabricating a paper-based sensing probe. The developed sensing method is achieved by simply adding the analyte to the CQD/AA probe without any complex surface modification. The cytotoxicity studies of the CQD revealed that at increasing doses of the CQD from 10 to 1000 μg/mL, U251MG cells viability was augmented (>100 % viability). The high biocompatible nature of the CQD enabled its application in invitro cell imaging. Additionally, other multifunctional applications of CQD are demonstrated by developing fluorescent ink and fluorescent polymer thin film.

Arumugam Selva Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jing Xuing: Visualization, Investigation. Annavaram Viswadevarayalu: Formal analysis, Visualization, Investigation. Yawen Rong: Visualization. Devaraj Sabarinathan: Formal analysis, Visualization, Investigation. Shujat Ali: Visualization, Investigation. Akwasi Akomeah Agyekum: Formal analysis, Visualization. Huanhuan Li: Visualization. Quansheng Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

AS gratefully acknowledges China Postdoctoral Science Foundation and Jiangsu University for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112788.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
